content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The older I get as a software engineer, the less impressed I am by clever code.
A few years ago, I loved writing code that made me feel smart. Dense abstractions. Fancy design patterns. Generic types nested inside more generic types. APIs that looked elegant — at least until I came back six months later and couldn’t remember what I was thinking.
These days, my philosophy is much simpler.
I build software like I’m going to maintain it for the next ten years.
Not because I know I actually will.
But because thinking that way changes every decision you make.
It changes how you structure your folders.
How you name your variables.
How you write documentation.
How you design APIs.
Even how you decide whether a feature should exist at all.
I’ve come to believe that software isn’t finished when it compiles.
It’s finished when Future You can understand it without swearing.
Software Ages Like Milk
People often compare software to buildings.
I don’t.
Buildings usually become more valuable with time if they’re maintained.
Software is more like a garden.
Leave it alone for a few months and weeds start appearing.
Dependencies become outdated.
Security vulnerabilities show up.
Frameworks release breaking changes.
Browsers change.
Operating systems evolve.
Databases improve.
Customers ask for new features.
The code that looked clean last year suddenly feels ancient.
That’s why writing software isn’t really about today’s requirements.
It’s about preparing for tomorrow’s changes.
The only constant in software is that it will change.
Future Derek Is a Different Developer
One of the funniest things about programming is that six months from now, you become a stranger to your own code.
You open a file.
Scroll down.
See a function.
Pause.
Then ask yourself:
“Who wrote this?”
The answer is uncomfortable.
You did.
I’ve had moments where I blamed previous developers for confusing code before realizing I was the previous developer.
That realization permanently changed my approach.
Now I don’t write code for Present Derek.
I write code for Future Derek.
Future Derek has forgotten the context.
Future Derek is probably tired.
Future Derek might be debugging production at two in the morning.
He deserves readable code.
Readability Is a Feature
People treat readability like it’s a nice bonus.
I think it’s a core feature.
Imagine buying a car whose engine only one mechanic in the world understands.
You wouldn’t call that good engineering.
Yet developers celebrate code that only its author can understand.
Simple code isn’t a sign of limited ability.
It’s usually a sign of maturity.
The best engineers I’ve worked with rarely write code that tries to impress other engineers.
They write code that quietly solves problems.
Ten years later, that’s the code people are still happy to maintain.
Clever Code Has an Expiration Date
Every engineer eventually discovers the temptation to be clever.
Compress five lines into one.
Invent a new abstraction.
Create a generic solution for a problem that exists exactly once.
At first it feels satisfying.
Until someone has to debug it.
I’ve learned to ask one question before introducing complexity:
“Will this still make sense in five years?”
If the answer is “probably not,” I usually choose the simpler approach.
Simple code survives.
Clever code often becomes technical debt.
Naming Things Is More Important Than Algorithms
Programming books joke that naming things is one of the hardest problems in computer science.
They’re right.
A function called process() tells you almost nothing.
A function called calculateMonthlyRevenue() tells you exactly what it does.
The computer doesn’t care.
Humans do.
I spend more time naming things today than I did earlier in my career.
Not because I’ve become slower.
Because I’ve realized names are documentation.
Every good name eliminates the need for another comment.
Comments Should Explain Why
I used to comment everything.
// Increment i
i++;That isn’t documentation.
That’s narration.
Modern code should explain what it’s doing through clear structure.
Comments should explain why something exists.
Why are we caching this?
Why is this query written differently?
Why can’t this value be null?
Why did we intentionally avoid a simpler implementation?
Those are the questions Future You won’t remember.
Architecture Should Feel Boring
I’ve stopped chasing exciting architectures.
Microservices.
Event sourcing.
CQRS.
Hexagonal architecture.
Plugin systems.
They’re all useful — in the right context.
But architecture isn’t about choosing the most sophisticated option.
It’s about choosing the simplest system that can survive future growth.
Sometimes that’s a monolith.
Sometimes that’s a modular monolith.
Sometimes that’s distributed services.
Good architecture rarely attracts attention.
Bad architecture always does.
Every Dependency Is a Promise
Adding a library takes seconds.
Removing it years later might take months.
Every dependency is another team making decisions for your software.
Another release schedule.
Another potential security issue.
Another API that might disappear.
I don’t avoid dependencies.
I just respect the cost of introducing them.
Whenever possible, I ask:
“Could we solve this ourselves with fifty lines instead of depending on fifty thousand?”
Sometimes the answer is yes.
Sometimes the answer is no.
But the question is worth asking.
Documentation Is Not Optional
Nobody likes writing documentation.
I didn’t either.
Until I realized I kept answering the same questions.
Documentation isn’t for users.
It’s for reducing future interruptions.
A README saves conversations.
Architecture diagrams save meetings.
API documentation saves debugging sessions.
Installation guides save frustration.
Good documentation compounds.
Like good code, it becomes more valuable over time.
I Design APIs Like Conversations
One of my favorite parts of software engineering is API design.
Whether it’s a REST API, an SDK, or a Rust library, I think about one thing.
Does this feel natural?
The best APIs don’t require memorization.
They guide you toward the correct solution.
When I started building developer tools, I realized something interesting.
Developers aren’t just using your software.
They’re communicating with it.
Every confusing function name is a confusing sentence.
Every awkward method chain feels like an awkward conversation.
Good APIs disappear.
Bad ones constantly remind you they exist.
Technical Debt Is a Loan
People often talk about technical debt like it’s inherently bad.
It isn’t.
Debt can help you move faster.
The problem isn’t borrowing.
The problem is pretending you don’t have to repay it.
Sometimes deadlines matter.
Sometimes shipping matters more than elegance.
That’s okay.
But I try to record every shortcut.
Future maintenance begins with honest engineering.
Testing Buys Confidence
I used to think tests slowed development.
Now I think the opposite.
Tests speed up change.
Without tests, every modification feels risky.
With tests, refactoring becomes routine.
A decade is a long time.
Requirements will change.
Features will evolve.
Developers will come and go.
Tests become the safety net that allows software to keep improving.
The Best Software Evolves
One mistake I made early in my career was believing good software starts perfect.
It doesn’t.
It starts useful.
Every version teaches you something.
Version 0.1 reveals missing features.
Version 0.5 reveals usability problems.
Version 1.0 reveals scaling challenges.
Version 3.0 reveals assumptions you didn’t know you made.
Great software isn’t built once.
It’s continuously refined.
Building Developer Tools Changed My Perspective
Working on developer tools taught me lessons I never learned building applications alone.
When you’re creating libraries or frameworks, every design decision affects other engineers.
You start asking different questions.
Can someone understand this without reading the source code?
Will this naming still make sense years from now?
Can this feature evolve without breaking existing users?
Those questions make you a better engineer everywhere else.
Even when building internal systems.
The Long Game
The technology industry moves fast.
Every week there’s a new framework.
A new JavaScript runtime.
Another AI model.
Another programming language feature.
It’s exciting.
But beneath all the hype, good engineering principles barely change.
Readable code.
Clear architecture.
Good documentation.
Reliable testing.
Thoughtful APIs.
Those ideas survived the last twenty years.
I suspect they’ll survive the next twenty too.
Software Is Never Really Finished
One lesson entrepreneurship taught me is that products evolve through relationships with users.
Software is no different.
Every bug report teaches you something.
Every feature request reveals another perspective.
Every issue uncovers assumptions.
Maintaining software isn’t a burden.
It’s the continuation of the design process.
The first release isn’t the finish line.
It’s the beginning of a conversation.
My Philosophy
When I write software today, I imagine opening the project a decade from now.
Maybe the technologies will have changed.
Maybe the programming language will look different.
Maybe I’ll have forgotten why I built it.
But I hope one thing remains true.
I hope the code still feels welcoming.
I hope the architecture still feels understandable.
I hope another developer can contribute without fear.
And I hope Future Me silently thanks Present Me instead of wondering what on earth I was thinking.
Because the best software isn’t the software that impresses people on launch day.
It’s the software that’s still enjoyable to work on years later.
Anyone can write code that works today.
The real challenge — the one I find most rewarding — is writing code that will still make sense tomorrow.
That’s why I build software like I’m going to maintain it for ten years.
Not because I know I will.
But because software deserves to outlive the excitement of writing it, and good engineering is measured not by how quickly you build something, but by how gracefully it survives the passage of time.
